paper_title: DU-VPT: Decomposed Uncertainty-Guided Visual Prompt Tuning for Test-Time Adaptation
email_title: DU-VPT: Decomposed Uncertainty-Guided Visual Prompt Tuning for Test-Time Adaptation

sections.summary: This paper proposes DU-VPT, a test-time adaptation (TTA) framework for Vision Transformers that (i) decomposes per-layer predictive uncertainty into aleatoric (input corruption) and epistemic (domain unfamiliarity) components without sampling/ensembling, (ii) diagnoses the likely shift type and its locus across the ViT depth, and (iii) injects targeted “structural” or “semantic” visual prompts only in affected layers. On ImageNet-C (severity 5), ImageNet-R, and ImageNet-Sketch with a ViT-B/16 backbone, DU-VPT reports absolute gains of 2.3–3.9% over the strongest included baseline while updating ~1% parameters, supported by ablations on selective versus uniform prompting, decomposition versus single uncertainty metrics, and prompts versus weight updates.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation
The central idea of layer-wise uncertainty decomposition to distinguish low-level corruptions from high-level domain shifts is interesting and intuitive for hierarchical ViT representations.
Using the decomposed signals to both select layers and choose between “structural” and “semantic” prompt types is a neat, targeted alternative to uniform prompting or indiscriminate weight updates.
The proposed aleatoric measure based on local patch-token consistency and the epistemic measure based on deviation from cached feature statistics enable single-pass diagnosis without sampling/ensembling.


Experimental rigor and validation
Comparisons include source, TENT/EATA, uniform VPT-Deep, and a layer-selective method (PALM), with three seeds reported and ablations that isolate key components: layer selection, decomposition, and prompt vs. weight-update trade-offs.
Catastrophic forgetting is explicitly measured; the prompt-based approach shows lower forgetting than weight updates.


Clarity of presentation
The overall pipeline (decomposition → diagnosis → targeted prompts) is clearly laid out, with an algorithmic flow and design motivations tied to ViT’s hierarchical feature encoding.
Ablations are well organized to support the claimed design choices.


Significance of contributions
The work aims to make TTA more interpretable and parameter-efficient by revealing where and why adaptation is needed, potentially informing future layer-aware or shift-type-aware adaptation strategies.
The proposed framework is modular and, in principle, extensible to other prompt variants or selection rules.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns
The epistemic uncertainty proxy ε_l = ||BN(z_l; μ_cal, σ_cal) − z_l|| is under-justified for LayerNorm-based ViTs and unusual in that it compares normalized features to raw features; it is unclear how this relates to epistemic uncertainty, whether statistics are computed per-dimension/token, and how scaling is handled.
The aleatoric measure based on neighboring patch-token similarity is applied uniformly across layers, but later-layer tokens are globally mixed by attention; the “local neighborhood” notion may become less meaningful with depth and requires more justification.
Shift-type diagnosis relies on fixed thresholds (τ_α, τ_ε) and coarse rules over thirds/halves of depth; sensitivity and robustness to these choices are not reported.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues
Several strong and ViT-specific TTA baselines are missing: Class-Conditional Feature Alignment (CFA), PCSR (QKV recalibration with domain token), MEMO (single-point marginal entropy), and more recent methods such as AttenDence or PLUTO. Notably, PCSR reports substantially higher ImageNet-C accuracy for ViT-B/16 (≈67.5% at severity 5) than the numbers here (≈51%), which calls for reconciling setups or adding direct comparisons.
Results on ImageNet-C are reported only at severity 5, whereas many works report averages across severities or mCE; the restricted setting makes it hard to situate progress.
The claimed shift-type diagnosis accuracy (85%) is not supported by a detailed protocol, ground truth, or analysis in the experiments section.
Compute/latency overhead is not reported; test-time per-sample prompt optimization across multiple layers can be nontrivial.


Clarity or presentation issues
Details on the construction and initialization of “structural” vs. “semantic” prompt banks per layer are sparse: Are these initialized randomly per deployment, shared across layers, or warmed up with calibration data?
The exact neighborhood definition for patch tokens and how it is updated across layers is underspecified; figures or schematics would help.


Missing related work or comparisons
The paper positions BN-based TTA as “inapplicable” to ViTs, yet several recent ViT-oriented methods (CFA, PCSR) explicitly address ViTs or LN-based architectures; these should be included and discussed thoroughly, with head-to-head comparisons where feasible.
Works addressing heterogeneity/mixed domains (e.g., FreDA) or attention-based regularization (AttenDence) are relevant alternatives and may offer orthogonal or complementary signals; discussion is limited.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation
The overall framework is coherent, but the two core uncertainty proxies deserve stronger theoretical or empirical grounding:
The ε_l formula compares a BN-normalized tensor (using cached μ, σ) against the raw z_l. This induces dimension- and scale-dependent biases. Clarify whether features are first mean-centered per token/layer or projected (e.g., via LN†) before comparison. A normalized distance (e.g., Mahalanobis or CMD-style statistics) may be more principled.
The α_l local-consistency measure presumes spatial coherence as a signature of aleatoric corruption. However, later layers’ tokens reflect global context; neighborhood similarity may not correspond to corruption-induced variance. Consider layer-varying neighborhood definitions, attention-aware neighborhoods, or reporting layer-wise behavior.


The rule-based diagnosis across layer thirds with fixed thresholds is simple and potentially brittle. A learnable or calibration-driven mapping from (α_l, ε_l) profiles to adaptation plans could improve robustness and portability.
The structural-loss design (local smoothness of features) risks over-smoothing details crucial for classification under some corruptions (e.g., edge-preserving noise). A discussion/ablation disentangling benefits vs. harms per corruption would strengthen the case.
Anti-forgetting via Fisher from a calibration set is reasonable, but “source-free” assumptions should explicitly acknowledge this calibration dependence.


Experimental evaluation assessment
The comparisons to TENT, EATA, VPT-Deep, and PALM are reasonable starting points, but they omit several relevant and stronger baselines for ViTs (CFA, PCSR). Given the large discrepancy in absolute ImageNet-C severity-5 numbers reported elsewhere, direct comparisons or an explanation of setup differences (pretraining data, data augmentation, corruption averaging protocol) are needed to contextualize the gains.
Reporting only severity 5 for ImageNet-C limits interpretability; providing averages across severities (or mCE) and per-corruption breakdowns would align with common practice and enable fairer cross-paper comparison.
Compute/latency: Test-time prompt optimization implies additional backward passes; report per-image or per-batch wall-clock (CPU/GPU), steps per sample, and memory overhead, and compare against included baselines. Parameter count alone is not a full measure of efficiency.
Diagnosis accuracy (85%) is mentioned but not substantiated with a labeled protocol (e.g., mapping corruption types to “low-level” vs. “semantic” ground-truth). Please include experimental details, confusion matrices, and effects of misdiagnosis on accuracy/robustness.
Robustness to label shift or mixed-domain batches (PTTA-like protocols) is not evaluated; some TTA methods are brittle under label shift. Even a small-scale study (e.g., Dirichlet-varying priors) would be informative.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)
CFA (Kojima et al., 2206.13951) directly tackles ViT TTA via distributional alignment using stored source statistics; DU-VPT’s ε_l resembles a per-layer distribution distance. A direct comparison and deeper discussion of per-layer statistics vs. class-conditional/global alignment is warranted.
PCSR (2512.12673) updates small per-layer QKV recalibration modules online and reports much higher ImageNet-C numbers for ViT-B/16 at severity 5; a fair head-to-head comparison (same backbone, corruption protocol, and reporting metric) would significantly strengthen claims.
MEMO (2110.09506) provides a single-sample augmentation-averaged entropy objective applicable to ViT; even if protocols differ, a comparison under a common batch setting would help.
FreDA (2411.15173) addresses mixed shifts by partitioning data; while orthogonal, acknowledging scenarios where DU-VPT’s layer-wise diagnosis might complement sample-wise partitioning would broaden impact.
SNAP (2511.15276) focuses on latency-efficient TTA and adapts to ViTs; DU-VPT could benefit from sparse-update strategies. At minimum, discuss compute trade-offs and compatibility.
AttenDence (2511.18925) uses attention-entropy regularization for ViTs; the proposed α_l signal could be augmented with attention-map diagnostics to better localize structural vs. semantic issues.


Discussion of broader impact and significance
The interpretability afforded by layer-wise decomposition and adaptation planning is a strength, potentially guiding debugging and design of ViT architectures for robustness.
Practical deployment requires careful consideration of calibration data access, compute budgets for online adaptation, and behavior under label shift and streaming protocols. Extending DU-VPT to these settings, or composing with memory/selection schemes, could amplify impact.
sections.questions_for_authors:
How are the thresholds τ_α and τ_ε selected and are they dataset- or model-specific? Please provide sensitivity analyses and, if possible, an automatic calibration procedure.
Please clarify the epistemic proxy: how is BN(.) applied to LayerNorm-based ViTs? Are μ_cal and σ_cal computed per-layer per-dimension across patch tokens? Why compare BN-normalized features to raw features rather than using a principled distance (e.g., Mahalanobis/CMD)?
How are “structural” and “semantic” prompt banks initialized and maintained? Are they shared across layers, per-layer distinct, and do they persist across the stream or reset episodically?
What is the per-sample/batch runtime and memory overhead of DU-VPT (forward for decomposition + backward for prompt update + forward for prediction), and how does it compare to PALM, VPT-Deep, and TENT/EATA on the same hardware?
You report ImageNet-C at severity 5 only; can you provide results averaged across severities (or mCE) and per-corruption breakdowns to align with standard reporting?
Could you add direct comparisons to ViT-oriented TTA baselines such as CFA and PCSR? If not feasible, please explain differences in setup that could account for the large discrepancy in absolute accuracies.
How is the “85% shift-type diagnosis accuracy” computed? What is the ground-truth labeling of shift type, and how does misdiagnosis affect downstream accuracy?
Does DU-VPT handle label shift or mixed-domain streams (PTTA)? If not, what failure modes do you observe and could the decomposition guide conservative updates in such scenarios?
How robust is α_l to the choice of neighborhood in deeper layers where tokens are globally mixed by attention? Have you tried attention-aware neighborhoods or multi-head attention similarity as alternatives?
Can the epistemic/aleatoric decomposition be calibrated (e.g., layer-wise normalization or temperature scaling) to reduce reliance on hard thresholds and improve cross-domain portability?
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    4.4/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            This paper presents a compelling and interpretable approach to TTA for ViTs by decomposing uncertainty across layers and applying targeted prompts accordingly. The core intuition is sound and the framework is modular, with ablations that support the benefit of selective, diagnosis-driven prompting over uniform prompts or indiscriminate weight updates. However, important technical details of the uncertainty proxies (especially the LayerNorm-consistent formulation of the epistemic metric) are insufficiently justified, and the diagnosis mechanism hinges on fixed thresholds without reported robustness. More critically, the empirical evaluation omits several strong and ViT-specific TTA baselines (e.g., CFA, PCSR), and the absolute accuracy on ImageNet-C (severity 5) appears substantially below the best-reported numbers in contemporaneous work, making it difficult to gauge true state-of-the-art standing. The focus on severity 5 alone, lack of compute/latency reporting, and limited analysis of diagnosis accuracy further limit the current strength of the evidence.
Overall, I find the idea promising and potentially impactful, especially for interpretable, parameter-efficient TTA. With stronger theoretical grounding of the decomposition, comprehensive comparisons to ViT-focused baselines, broader ImageNet-C reporting, and clear efficiency metrics, this work could be competitive for ICLR. In its current form, I lean toward rejection, but I encourage the authors to address the above points, as the direction is worthwhile and the preliminary results are encouraging.
